walkthrough.title: Choose Your Super Hero
walkthrough.text: Welcome to the DC Universe! First things first, each player gets dealt a random oversized Super Hero card. For your first game, use Batman, Superman, Green Lantern, Wonder Woman, and The Flash. Set aside any extras. Your Super Hero gives you a unique ability you can use every turn, so take a moment to read yours. Keep it face up in front of you where everyone can see it.

walkthrough.title: Build Your Starting Deck
walkthrough.text: Each player starts with an identical deck of ten cards: seven Punch cards and three Vulnerability cards. The Punches are your bread and butter. Each one gives you +1 Power, which is the currency you use to buy better cards. The Vulnerabilities? Those are dead weight. They do absolutely nothing when you draw them. Think of them as the things that slow a hero down. You will want to get rid of them as soon as you can. Shuffle your ten cards face down into a personal deck.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Main Deck
walkthrough.text: Now find the main deck. This is the big stack of 114 cards that includes Heroes, Villains, Super Powers, Equipment, and Locations. These are all the powerful cards you will be adding to your deck throughout the game. Give the main deck a thorough shuffle and place it face down in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. Important: do not mix in Punches, Vulnerabilities, Kicks, Weaknesses, Super-Villains, or the oversized Super Hero cards. Those all have their own places.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Line-Up
walkthrough.text: Flip the top five cards from the main deck face up in a row next to it. This row is called the Line-Up, and it is the marketplace where you will shop for new cards on your turn. Every time someone buys a card from the Line-Up, it gets refilled at the end of that player's turn. The Line-Up is always changing, so you never know what powerful cards might show up next.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Super-Villain Stack
walkthrough.text: Time to assemble the rogues gallery. There are twelve Super-Villain cards in the box. For a standard game, you will use eight. Find Ra's al Ghul and set him aside. He always goes on top. Shuffle the remaining eleven Super-Villains face down, then randomly remove four of them from the game without looking. Stack the remaining seven face down, then place Ra's al Ghul face up on top. He is the first Super-Villain the heroes will face. Place the stack at the end of the Line-Up.

walkthrough.title: Place the Kick Stack
walkthrough.text: Find all sixteen Kick cards and stack them face up next to the Super-Villain stack. Kicks are always available to buy during the game. Each one costs 3 Power and gives you +2 Power when played. Think of Kicks as a reliable upgrade when the Line-Up does not have anything good you can afford. They are a solid way to power up your deck early on.

walkthrough.title: Place the Weakness Stack
walkthrough.text: Stack all twenty Weakness cards face up in their own pile near the other stacks. You never buy Weaknesses. They only enter your deck through nasty Attack effects from Villains and Super-Villains. Each Weakness subtracts 1 Victory Point from your final score, so they are bad news. When you get one, it goes into your discard pile and clogs up your deck. You will want to destroy them when you get the chance.

walkthrough.title: Draw Your Starting Hand
walkthrough.text: Each player shuffles their personal ten-card deck and draws five cards. This is your opening hand. You will probably see four Punches and one Vulnerability, or maybe three Punches and two Vulnerabilities. Not the most exciting hand, but every hero has to start somewhere. One more thing: if anyone is playing as The Flash, that player goes first. Otherwise, pick a random starting player. Play goes clockwise. Let's get heroic!

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview: Play and Buy
walkthrough.text: Your turn has two phases. First, you play cards from your hand. Each card you play gives you Power, draws you extra cards, triggers Attacks, or activates other abilities. Resolve each card's text as you play it. Second, once you have played everything you want, total up your Power and go shopping. You can buy cards from the Line-Up, buy Kick cards, or defeat the face-up Super-Villain. You can buy as many cards as you can afford in a single turn. Any unspent Power is lost at the end of your turn.

walkthrough.title: Power: Your Currency
walkthrough.text: Power is the currency of the DC universe. Every card you play that says +Power adds to your total for the turn. Your starting Punch cards give +1 Power each. Kicks give +2. The bigger, flashier cards in the main deck can give +3, +4, or even more. When you want to buy a card, check the cost number in the lower left corner. You can buy any combination of cards whose total cost does not exceed your Power for the turn. For example, if you have 6 Power, you could buy one card costing 6, two cards costing 3 each, or one costing 4 and one costing 2.

walkthrough.title: Buying Cards and Where They Go
walkthrough.text: When you buy or gain a card, it goes immediately into your discard pile, not your hand. You will not get to use it until your discard pile gets shuffled back into your deck and you eventually draw it. This is the fundamental rhythm of deck building: buy a card now, benefit from it later. The Line-Up cards, Kicks, and the top Super-Villain are all available targets. The main deck itself and the Weakness stack are never available to buy from directly.

walkthrough.title: Card Types and What They Do
walkthrough.text: There are several card types in the game and each plays differently. Heroes like Kid Flash and Hawkman provide Power and card draw. Super Powers like Heat Vision and Super Speed give you Power plus abilities like destroying cards. Equipment like The Cape and Cowl and Nth Metal grant Power and often have Defense abilities. Villains like The Joker and Scarecrow give you Power but often come with Attack abilities that hurt your opponents. Locations are special: when you play one, it stays face up in front of you for the rest of the game with an Ongoing effect that triggers each turn.

walkthrough.title: Attacks and Defenses
walkthrough.text: Some cards have Attack abilities that affect all other players. When you play an Attack card, each opponent must deal with the effect unless they have a Defense. Defense abilities are found on certain Super Power and Equipment cards. A defending player discards their Defense card to avoid the Attack entirely and usually gets a bonus like drawing cards. Important: each player can only play one Defense card per Attack. If you do not have a Defense, you must suffer the full effect. Attacks often force opponents to discard cards or gain Weaknesses.

walkthrough.title: Defeating Super-Villains
walkthrough.text: Super-Villains sit on their own stack with the current one face up. To defeat a Super-Villain, you need enough Power during your turn. They range in cost from 8 to 12, so you need a strong hand. When you defeat one, take the card and put it in your discard pile. Super-Villain cards are worth big Victory Points and have powerful abilities when you play them from your hand later. You can only defeat one Super-Villain per turn. The next one in the stack stays face down until your turn ends.

walkthrough.title: First Appearance Attacks
walkthrough.text: Here is where things get dramatic. When a new Super-Villain is flipped face up at the end of a turn, their First Appearance Attack immediately hits every player. Ra's al Ghul does not have one since he starts the game face up, but every other Super-Villain does. These attacks can be devastating: The Joker makes everyone pass a card to the player on their left, Lex Luthor forces everyone to gain Weaknesses for each Villain in the Line-Up, and Parallax makes everyone discard all cards costing 2 or less. You can defend against First Appearance Attacks if you have a Defense card in your hand.

walkthrough.title: Locations and Ongoing Effects
walkthrough.text: Location cards like The Batcave, Arkham Asylum, and Titans Tower work differently from other cards. When you play a Location, it stays face up in front of you permanently. It has an Ongoing ability that triggers once per turn when you play a specific card type. For example, The Batcave lets you draw a card when you play your first Equipment each turn. Arkham Asylum draws you a card when you play your first Villain each turn. You can have multiple Locations in play at once, and each one triggers independently.

walkthrough.title: Destroying Cards
walkthrough.text: Some cards let you destroy a card from your hand, discard pile, or even the Line-Up. Destroying a card removes it from the game permanently. This is incredibly valuable because your Vulnerability and Weakness cards are dragging your deck down. Every time you destroy a dead card, your future hands get stronger. Cards like Heat Vision and Nth Metal have destroy abilities. Destroyed Kicks and Weaknesses do not return to their stacks. Think of destroying as the ultimate way to tune your deck.

walkthrough.title: End of Turn Cleanup
walkthrough.text: When you are done playing cards and buying, three things happen. First, place all played cards and any remaining hand cards into your discard pile. Unspent Power is lost. Draw a new hand of five cards. Second, refill any empty slots in the Line-Up from the top of the main deck. Do not refill slots immediately when you buy a card; wait until your turn is completely over. Third, if the top card of the Super-Villain stack is face down, flip it face up and read its First Appearance Attack aloud. Then the next player takes their turn.

walkthrough.title: Reshuffling Your Deck
walkthrough.text: Do not reshuffle your discard pile just because your deck runs out. Only reshuffle when you actually need to draw, discard, or reveal a card from your deck and there are no cards left. At that point, shuffle your entire discard pile and it becomes your new deck. This means you will cycle through your deck multiple times during the game, and the new powerful cards you have been buying will start showing up in your hands.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends immediately when one of two things happens: you cannot flip up a new Super-Villain because the stack is empty, or you cannot refill all five slots of the Line-Up because the main deck runs out. When the game ends, every player counts the Victory Points on all cards in their deck, including their hand, discard pile, and any cards in play. The number in the star icon on each card is its VP value. Then subtract 1 VP for each Weakness card in your deck. The player with the highest total wins. In case of a tie, the player with more Super-Villain cards wins.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Gotham
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with three players. Player 1 is Batman, Player 2 is Wonder Woman, and Player 3 is Superman. We will show you different actions, common mistakes, and how the turn cycle works. Follow along even if your group has different heroes. Ready to save the world? Let's go.

walkthrough.title: The Starting Situation
walkthrough.text: Everyone has shuffled their ten-card starting decks and drawn five cards. The Line-Up has five random cards from the main deck. Ra's al Ghul sits face up on top of the Super-Villain stack, costing 8 Power to defeat. The Kick stack and Weakness stack are nearby. Each player has their Super Hero card face up in front of them. Let's see what everyone drew.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 (Batman): Opening Hand
walkthrough.text: Batman draws four Punches and one Vulnerability. A pretty typical opening hand. Batman plays all four Punches for a total of 4 Power. The Vulnerability just sits there doing nothing. Now Batman checks the Line-Up. There is a Hero card costing 3 and an Equipment card costing 4. Batman's Super Hero ability gives +1 Power for each Equipment played during the turn, so Equipment cards are extra valuable. Batman buys the cost-4 Equipment and puts it in the discard pile. That leaves 0 Power remaining.

walkthrough.title: Batman's End of Turn
walkthrough.text: Batman discards all played cards and the Vulnerability into the discard pile, along with the newly purchased Equipment. Batman draws five new cards from the deck. Since one slot in the Line-Up is empty, a new card is flipped from the main deck to fill it. The Super-Villain stack still shows Ra's al Ghul face up, so no First Appearance Attack happens.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 (Wonder Woman): Opening Hand
walkthrough.text: Wonder Woman draws three Punches and two Vulnerabilities. A weak hand with only 3 Power. The Line-Up does not have anything great for 3 Power, so Wonder Woman decides to buy a Kick card instead. Kicks always cost 3 and give +2 Power when played. That is a solid upgrade over a Punch. The Kick goes into Wonder Woman's discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Why Kicks Are a Good Fallback
walkthrough.text: Buying a Kick when you cannot afford anything better is a smart play. It costs only 3 Power and provides +2 Power when you play it later, which is double what a Punch gives you. Early in the game, your Power totals are low, so Kicks bridge the gap until you can afford the bigger cards in the Line-Up. Never waste a turn doing nothing if you can at least grab a Kick.

walkthrough.title: Player 3 (Superman): Opening Hand
walkthrough.text: Superman draws five Punches. No Vulnerabilities at all! That is the best possible starting hand: 5 Power. Superman scans the Line-Up and spots a Super Power card costing 5. Superman's Super Hero ability gives +1 Power the first time a card tells you to draw cards during your turn. Super Powers that draw cards would be especially valuable. Superman buys the cost-5 card and sends it to the discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Forgetting Your Super Hero
walkthrough.text: Here is a mistake new players make constantly. Player 3 almost forgot to check their Super Hero ability! Superman's power gives bonus Power or draws when certain card types are played. Every Super Hero has a different ability, and they are free to use every turn. Before you finish your turn, always glance at your Super Hero card. Batman gets bonuses for Equipment, Wonder Woman gets bonuses when card types are different, Cyborg benefits from Super Powers and Equipment, and so on. Forgetting your Super Hero ability is like leaving free points on the table.

walkthrough.title: Round Continues: Batman's Second Turn
walkthrough.text: Batman draws five cards from the remaining deck and gets three Punches, one Vulnerability, and the Equipment card purchased last turn. Batman plays the Equipment first, getting +2 Power from the card plus +1 Power from the Batman Super Hero ability for playing Equipment. Then Batman plays the three Punches for another +3 Power. That is 6 total Power. Batman spots a Villain in the Line-Up costing 5 that has an Attack ability. Batman buys it with Power to spare.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Playing Cards in the Wrong Order
walkthrough.text: Wait, did you notice something? Batman played the Equipment card before the Punches. That is important! The order you play cards can matter. Some cards have effects that interact with other cards you have already played or will play. For example, if you had a card that says 'If you play three or more cards with different names, +3 Power,' you would want to play diverse cards first. Always read your cards and think about sequencing before you start slapping cards down.

walkthrough.title: Wonder Woman Faces an Attack
walkthrough.text: Batman's new Villain has an Attack: each foe gains a Weakness. Wonder Woman checks her hand. She does not have any Defense cards yet, so she must take a Weakness from the Weakness stack and put it in her discard pile. Superman also has no Defense, so he takes a Weakness too. Those Weaknesses will clog up their decks and cost them 1 VP each at end of game.

walkthrough.title: Why Defense Cards Matter
walkthrough.text: This is a great lesson from the Attack. Defense cards like Super Speed and Bulletproof are not just nice to have. They can save you from gaining Weaknesses, discarding important cards, or worse. Some Defense cards even give you a bonus when you use them, like drawing two extra cards. Picking up a Defense card early gives you insurance against the super-villains and your opponents' Attack cards for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Wonder Woman Builds Up
walkthrough.text: Wonder Woman's second hand has three Punches and the Kick card. That is 3 + 2 = 5 Power. Wonder Woman's Super Hero ability triggers when she plays three or more cards with different names and cost 1 or more, granting +3 Power. Unfortunately, all the Punches have the same name, so the ability does not trigger yet. Wonder Woman buys a Hero card costing 4 from the Line-Up and saves 1 Power. She spots a Punch-cost card but decides against it. With 1 Power left, she cannot buy anything else, so the Power is lost.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Buying vs Defeating Confusion
walkthrough.text: Here is another rookie mistake. Player 2 looks at the Super-Villain stack and asks if she can buy Ra's al Ghul. The answer is no, you do not buy Super-Villains. You defeat them. The term might seem like the same thing, but the rulebook is specific: you defeat Super-Villains by paying their Power cost. Some card abilities reference 'buying' and 'defeating' separately. If a card says 'when you buy a card,' that does not apply when you defeat a Super-Villain. And vice versa. Keep those terms straight.

walkthrough.title: Superman Goes Big
walkthrough.text: Superman's second hand includes the cost-5 Super Power bought earlier, two Punches, and two Vulnerabilities. Superman plays the Super Power first, getting +3 Power. Superman's Super Hero ability says the first time a card tells you to draw one or more cards during your turn, draw an additional card. If this Super Power included a draw effect, Superman would get that bonus. Superman then plays the two Punches for +2 more Power, totaling 5 Power. The Vulnerabilities remain unplayed. Superman buys a cost-5 card from the Line-Up.

walkthrough.title: Eyeing the Super-Villain
walkthrough.text: After a few more turns, the players have been powering up their decks. Batman has a hand generating 9 Power. Ra's al Ghul costs 8. Batman decides to defeat him! Batman takes Ra's al Ghul and puts him in the discard pile. Ra's is worth 5 Victory Points and his card text says '+3 Power. At the end of your turn, put this card on the bottom of its owner's deck.' This means when Batman later draws and plays Ra's, he gets +3 Power and then the card goes to the bottom of his deck rather than the discard pile.

walkthrough.title: A New Super-Villain Appears
walkthrough.text: At the end of Batman's turn, the next Super-Villain in the stack is flipped face up. It is a new villain with a First Appearance Attack! The Attack text is read aloud, and every player, including Batman, must deal with it. If the attack says each player gains a Weakness, everyone takes one. If anyone has a Defense card in their hand right now, they can use it to avoid the attack. This is why holding onto a Defense card between turns can be a lifesaver.

walkthrough.title: Deck Cycling: Your Engine Grows
walkthrough.text: By now, players have gone through their starting decks and reshuffled. The new cards they bought are showing up in their hands alongside the original Punches and Vulnerabilities. This is the magic of deck building. Every card you bought is now part of your engine. Batman is drawing Equipment and triggering his Super Hero bonus. Superman is playing Super Powers and drawing extra cards. Wonder Woman is buying diverse card types to trigger her ability. Each cycle through the deck gets more powerful.

walkthrough.title: Destroying Vulnerabilities
walkthrough.text: Superman draws Heat Vision, which says '+3 Power. You may destroy a card in your hand or discard pile.' Superman immediately uses it to destroy one of the two Vulnerabilities in hand. That Vulnerability is removed from the game forever. Superman's deck is now nine cards instead of ten, and the ratio of good cards to dead cards just improved. Every time you destroy a Vulnerability or Weakness, your average hand gets stronger. Prioritize cards with destroy abilities, especially early in the game.

walkthrough.title: The Game Takes Shape
walkthrough.text: Play continues turn by turn. The Line-Up keeps refreshing with new cards. Super-Villains get defeated one by one, triggering First Appearance Attacks each time. Players' decks grow bigger and more powerful. The key decisions every turn are the same: which cards to play and in what order, and which cards to buy. Do you grab a high-VP card to boost your end score? Or a card with a strong ability to make future turns even better? That tension is what makes the game exciting.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen a full turn cycle, watched players make purchases, deal with Attacks, defeat a Super-Villain, and manage their decks. You have also spotted three common mistakes: forgetting your Super Hero ability, not thinking about card play order, and confusing buying with defeating. You are ready to play a full game on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Suit Up
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First -- check out General Tips for strategy basics like when to buy Kicks versus saving for bigger cards.

Second -- reference the Appendix for a quick guide to card types, Super Hero abilities, and key terms.

And most importantly -- ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun, heroes!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the biggest beginner mistakes. First, forgetting your Super Hero ability. It is free every turn and can swing your Power by 1 to 3 points. Second, buying cards that do not match your Super Hero's strengths. Batman wants Equipment, Cyborg wants Super Powers and Equipment, Wonder Woman wants variety. Third, ignoring Vulnerabilities. Those three dead cards in your starting deck reduce your average hand quality every time you draw them. Destroy them as soon as you can. Fourth, hoarding Power hoping for something better next turn. Unspent Power is lost, so buy something or you wasted it.

walkthrough.title: Know When to Buy Kicks
walkthrough.text: Kicks are the unsung heroes of the early game. If you cannot afford anything better in the Line-Up, a Kick for 3 Power is almost always the right call. Each Kick gives +2 Power when played, which is a significant upgrade over your starting Punches. However, do not over-invest in Kicks. Once your deck has three or four, you are better off saving up for more powerful main deck cards. Kicks are a bridge, not a destination.

walkthrough.title: Play to Your Super Hero
walkthrough.text: Your Super Hero ability is the single biggest advantage you have, and it is free every turn. Build your deck around it. Batman gets +1 Power per Equipment played, so stock up on Equipment cards. Cyborg gets +1 Power for Super Powers and draws a card for Equipment, so he wants both. Wonder Woman gets +3 Power when she plays three or more differently named cards costing 1 or more, so she wants variety. The Flash draws an extra card the first time a card tells you to draw, so he wants cards with draw effects. Green Lantern gets +3 Power for three differently named cards, similar to Wonder Woman. Let your Super Hero guide your purchases.

walkthrough.title: Deck Thinning Is King
walkthrough.text: The single most powerful strategy in any deck building game is making your deck smaller and better. Every time you destroy a Vulnerability or Weakness, your deck shrinks by one card, and your chances of drawing your best cards go up. If you start with ten cards and destroy all three Vulnerabilities, you now have a seven-card deck cycling faster with no dead draws. Cards like Heat Vision, Nth Metal, and certain Super Powers let you destroy cards. Prioritize them. A lean deck is a mean deck.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Super-Villain Attempt
walkthrough.text: Super-Villains are the big trophies of the game, worth 5 to 12 Victory Points each. But you need 8 to 12 Power in a single hand to defeat them. Do not rush for a Super-Villain before your deck is ready. If you have to dump your entire hand's Power into defeating a Super-Villain and buy nothing else, you missed the chance to also buy a strong card from the Line-Up. The ideal turn is defeating a Super-Villain and still having 2 to 4 Power left to grab a Line-Up card. Build your deck's Power ceiling first, then go for the big defeats.

walkthrough.title: Attacks Are Opportunities
walkthrough.text: Do not shy away from Villain cards with Attack abilities. Yes, they cost a bit more, but they provide Power and they disrupt your opponents every time you play them. Scarecrow makes foes gain Weaknesses. The Joker forces opponents to discard or let you draw. Bane makes foes choose and discard. Every Attack that forces Weaknesses on opponents is effectively worth double: you get the Power and VP from the card, and your opponents lose deck quality and end-game VP. If you can get a Defense card early to protect yourself from retaliation, even better.

walkthrough.title: Location Cards Are Long-Term Investments
walkthrough.text: Location cards like The Batcave, Arkham Asylum, and Titans Tower are some of the most valuable cards in the game. They stay in play permanently and give you a free effect every single turn. The Batcave draws you a card when you play your first Equipment each turn. If you play Equipment often, that is one extra card every turn for the rest of the game. The earlier you get a Location, the more turns it works for you. Prioritize Locations when they show up in the Line-Up, especially if they match your deck's card types.

walkthrough.title: Keep an Eye on the End Game
walkthrough.text: The game ends when the Super-Villain stack empties or the main deck cannot refill the Line-Up. Pay attention to how many Super-Villains remain and how thick the main deck is. When the end is near, shift your focus from building your deck to maximizing Victory Points. Buy the highest VP cards you can, even if their abilities are not great. A 5 VP card with a mediocre ability is still 5 points. In the final turns, VP value matters more than card synergy. Also, avoid picking up any Weaknesses in the late game if possible, since each one costs you a point and you may not have time to destroy it.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Forgetting your Super Hero ability (free every turn!)
Buying cards that don't match your Super Hero's strengths
Ignoring Vulnerabilities -- destroy them ASAP
Hoarding Power -- unspent Power is lost at end of turn

summary.title: Know When to Buy Kicks
bullets: Kicks (cost 3, +2 Power) are great early upgrades
Always buy a Kick if you can't afford anything better
Stop after 3-4 Kicks -- save Power for bigger main deck cards
Kicks are a bridge, not a destination

summary.title: Play to Your Super Hero
bullets: Batman = Equipment, Cyborg = Super Powers + Equipment
Wonder Woman / Green Lantern = card name variety
The Flash = card draw effects, Superman = high-Power cards
Let your Super Hero guide every purchase decision

summary.title: Deck Thinning Is King
bullets: Destroy Vulnerabilities and Weaknesses to shrink your deck
Smaller deck = draw your best cards more often
Prioritize cards with destroy abilities (Heat Vision, Nth Metal)
A 7-card deck with no dead draws crushes a 15-card bloated deck

summary.title: Timing Your Super-Villain Attempt
bullets: Super-Villains cost 8-12 Power and are worth 5-12 VP
Don't rush -- build your deck's Power ceiling first
Ideal turn: defeat Super-Villain AND buy a Line-Up card
Big VP swing, but only when your hand can handle it

summary.title: Attacks Are Opportunities
bullets: Villain cards with Attacks disrupt opponents every time played
Scarecrow = foes gain Weaknesses, Joker = discard or you draw
Attacks that give Weaknesses provide double value (Power for you, -VP for foes)
Pair with a Defense card for protection from retaliation

summary.title: Location Cards Are Long-Term Investments
bullets: Locations stay in play permanently with Ongoing effects
Batcave = draw on Equipment, Arkham Asylum = draw on Villain
Earlier purchase = more turns of free value
Prioritize Locations that match your deck's card types

summary.title: Keep an Eye on the End Game
bullets: Game ends when Super-Villain stack or main deck empties
Late game: prioritize raw VP value over card synergy
Avoid gaining Weaknesses near end -- no time to destroy them
Track remaining Super-Villains and main deck thickness

walkthrough.title: Aggressive Deck Thinning
walkthrough.text: The strongest strategy in the DC Deck Building Game is making your deck as small and powerful as possible. Your starting deck has ten cards, and three of them are Vulnerabilities that do nothing. If you can destroy all three Vulnerabilities in the first few cycles, you end up with a seven-card deck that draws five at a time. That means you see over seventy percent of your deck every hand. Every new powerful card you buy has a massive impact because you draw it almost every turn. Prioritize cards with destroy abilities like Heat Vision, Nth Metal, and Power Ring. Even using a destroy to remove a Punch later in the game is strong if it means you draw your best cards more consistently.

walkthrough.title: The Power Curve
walkthrough.text: Understanding the Power curve is key to winning. In the early game, you generate 3 to 5 Power per hand. Mid-game, you should be hitting 7 to 10. Late game, you want 10 to 15 or more to defeat Super-Villains and grab the most expensive Line-Up cards. The way to climb this curve quickly is to prioritize cards that give the most Power relative to their cost. A card costing 3 that gives +2 Power is great early. A card costing 5 that gives +3 Power plus a draw effect is even better because drawing extra cards means more Power in the same hand. Cards that multiply Power, like some Super-Villain abilities, are devastating in the late game.

walkthrough.title: Card Draw Combos
walkthrough.text: Card draw is the most underrated mechanic in the game. Every extra card you draw is more Power, more options, and more chances to trigger your Super Hero ability. The Flash's Super Hero power is the best example: the first time a card tells you to draw one or more cards, draw an extra card. With cards like Kid Flash, which says 'Draw a card,' The Flash draws two instead, then can play those drawn cards immediately for more Power or more draw effects. Chaining draw effects creates explosive turns where you play seven or eight cards from a starting hand of five.

walkthrough.title: Location Control
walkthrough.text: Locations are the most efficient cards in the game because they trigger every single turn for free after you play them once. The Batcave draws a card when you play your first Equipment each turn. If your deck has six Equipment cards, that is a guaranteed extra draw almost every turn. Arkham Asylum draws a card when you play your first Villain each turn. Titans Tower draws a card when you play your first Hero each turn. Stacking multiple Locations creates insane draw engines. If you control The Batcave and Titans Tower, and you play a Hero and an Equipment each turn, that is two free draws every turn. Get Locations early and build your deck to trigger them reliably.

walkthrough.title: Timing Super-Villain Defeats
walkthrough.text: Defeating Super-Villains is not just about having enough Power. The timing matters enormously. When you defeat a Super-Villain, the next one flips up and triggers a First Appearance Attack on everyone. If the next Super-Villain has a brutal attack, like Parallax making everyone discard all cards costing 2 or less, you might want to wait until you have a Defense card in hand or your opponents have juicy hands to lose. Conversely, if you suspect the next Super-Villain has a mild attack, defeating one right before your weakest opponent's turn can be devastating. Also consider that defeating the last Super-Villain ends the game immediately if the stack empties.

walkthrough.title: The Weakness Weapon
walkthrough.text: Attacks that force Weaknesses on opponents are one of the most powerful strategies in the game. Each Weakness is worth -1 VP at end of game, effectively a 2-point swing compared to you gaining +1 VP. Cards like Scarecrow force each foe to gain a Weakness. If you play Scarecrow three times over the course of the game against two opponents, that is six Weaknesses distributed, costing them a combined 6 VP. Meanwhile, you still get Scarecrow's +2 Power every time you play it. Stack multiple Attack cards to maximize disruption. If opponents are forced to spend turns destroying Weaknesses instead of buying strong cards, you have gained a massive tempo advantage.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Line-Up
walkthrough.text: Advanced players do not just look at what they can buy this turn. They look at what their opponents might buy next turn. If there is a perfect card for your opponent's strategy sitting in the Line-Up, consider buying it yourself even if it is not ideal for your deck. Denying your opponent a key Location or a strong synergy card can be worth more than buying something that fits your plan slightly better. Similarly, when you buy a card from the Line-Up, a new random card will replace it. Sometimes holding off on a purchase keeps the Line-Up unfavorable for your opponents. This kind of meta-thinking separates good players from great ones.

walkthrough.title: Suicide Squad: The Scaling Gambit
walkthrough.text: The Suicide Squad cards deserve special mention. There are six copies in the main deck, and their VP scales quadratically: one copy is worth 1 VP, two copies are worth 4 VP total, three copies are worth 9 VP total, and so on. Six copies would be worth 36 VP, which is almost impossible but illustrates the point. If you spot a Suicide Squad in the Line-Up and you already have one, grabbing another is a huge priority. Even two copies are worth 4 VP for relatively cheap cards. But be warned: if an opponent realizes you are collecting them, they might snatch one to deny you the scaling bonus. The Suicide Squad strategy is high-risk, high-reward, and always exciting.

walkthrough.title: End-Game VP Maximization
walkthrough.text: In the last few turns of the game, shift your priorities entirely to Victory Points. Stop buying cards for their abilities and start buying cards for their VP value. A cost-7 card worth 3 VP with a mediocre ability is better than a cost-3 card worth 1 VP with a great ability when the game is about to end. Pay attention to the Super-Villain stack and the main deck thickness. When there are only one or two Super-Villains left, every turn could be the last. Similarly, if the main deck is thin and multiple cards are being bought each round, the game could end from the Line-Up not refilling. In these final moments, every VP matters.

walkthrough.title: Hero-Specific Advanced Play
walkthrough.text: Each Super Hero enables a different advanced strategy. Batman should build an Equipment-heavy deck and pair it with The Batcave for double value. Cyborg wants a mix of Super Powers and Equipment, essentially getting value from two card types at once. Wonder Woman and Green Lantern both reward card diversity, so they should aim for a balanced deck with at least one of every card type to reliably trigger their +3 Power bonus. The Flash wants every draw effect he can find, chaining draws into massive turns. Superman benefits from raw high-Power cards since his ability generates extra draws. Identify your hero's optimal deck composition early and draft accordingly.

summary.title: Aggressive Deck Thinning
bullets: Destroy all 3 Vulnerabilities ASAP for a lean 7-card deck
7-card deck means you see 70%+ of your cards every hand
Prioritize destroy abilities: Heat Vision, Nth Metal, Power Ring
Even destroying Punches late-game improves hand quality

summary.title: The Power Curve
bullets: Early game: 3-5 Power per hand, Mid: 7-10, Late: 10-15+
Best buys: high Power-to-cost ratio + draw effects
Draw effects = more cards in hand = more Power per turn
Power-multiplying Super-Villain abilities dominate late game

summary.title: Card Draw Combos
summary.text: Card draw is underrated -- every extra card = more Power and options
bullets: Chain draw effects: play 7-8 cards from a starting hand of 5
The Flash + draw cards = explosive multi-draw turns
Kid Flash, Super Speed, and Heroes with draw powers are premium picks
More cards drawn = more chances to trigger Super Hero ability

summary.title: Location Control
bullets: Locations trigger every turn for free after being played once
Batcave + Equipment-heavy deck = guaranteed extra draw per turn
Stacking multiple Locations creates insane draw engines
Get Locations early -- more turns = more cumulative value

summary.title: Timing Super-Villain Defeats
bullets: Consider the next Super-Villain's First Appearance Attack before defeating the current one
Wait for a Defense card in hand if you suspect a brutal attack
Defeating right before a weak opponent's turn maximizes disruption
Defeating the last Super-Villain can end the game immediately

Scarecrow played 3x vs 2 opponents = 6 distributed Weaknesses
Forces opponents to waste turns destroying Weaknesses = tempo advantage
Stack multiple Attack cards for maximum disruption

summary.title: Reading the Line-Up

Consider what opponents need, not just what you need
Sometimes holding off keeps the Line-Up unfavorable for opponents
Meta-thinking separates good players from great ones

summary.title: Suicide Squad: The Scaling Gambit
bullets: 6 copies in main deck -- VP scales quadratically (n-squared)
1 copy = 1 VP, 2 = 4, 3 = 9, 4 = 16 VP total
Huge priority to grab multiples if you already have one
High-risk: opponents may snatch copies to deny your scaling

bullets: Final turns: buy for VP value, not card ability

summary.title: Hero-Specific Advanced Play
bullets: Batman: Equipment-heavy + The Batcave
Cyborg: Super Powers + Equipment dual-type value
Wonder Woman / Green Lantern: diverse card names for +3 Power
The Flash: chain draw effects; Superman: high-Power cards + draw synergy

entries.definition: The currency used to buy cards from the Line-Up, Kicks, or to defeat Super-Villains. Generated by playing cards during your turn. Unspent Power is lost at end of turn.
The star-shaped number on each card that determines your final score. Total all VP in your deck at game end. Weakness cards subtract 1 VP each. Highest total wins.
The row of five face-up cards from the main deck available for purchase. Refilled at the end of each player's turn. The Line-Up does not include the Kick, Super-Villain, or Weakness stacks.

The 114-card shuffled deck containing Heroes, Villains, Super Powers, Equipment, and Locations. Cards are drawn from it to fill the Line-Up. Does not include Punches, Vulnerabilities, Kicks, Weaknesses, or Super-Villains.

Each player's face-up pile of used cards. Bought and gained cards go here. At end of turn, all played and unplayed hand cards go here. Shuffled into a new deck only when you need to draw, discard, or reveal from an empty deck.
Remove a card permanently from the game. Destroyed cards go to a face-up pile away from the play area. Destroyed Kicks and Weaknesses do not return to their stacks. Key way to improve deck quality.
Acquire a card at no Power cost due to a card effect. Gained cards go to your discard pile unless stated otherwise. Distinct from buying (which costs Power).
The term used specifically for acquiring Super-Villains by paying their Power cost. Distinct from buying. Card effects that reference 'buying' do not apply to defeating Super-Villains and vice versa.
The number in the lower-left corner of a card, indicating how much Power is needed to buy (or defeat) it. Starting cards (Punch, Vulnerability) have a cost of 0.
A card type found in the main deck. Heroes typically provide Power and card draw effects. Examples: Kid Flash (draw a card), The Fastest Man Alive (draw two cards), Hawkman.

A card type found in the main deck. Villains provide Power and often have Attack abilities. Examples: The Joker (+2 Power, foe discards or you draw), Scarecrow (+2 Power, Attack: each foe gains a Weakness), Bane (+2 Power, Attack: foe discards).

Powerful Villain cards on their own stack. Cost 8-12 to defeat. Worth 5-12 VP. Have First Appearance Attacks when flipped face up. When played from hand later, use the top text only (not the First Appearance Attack). Super-Villain is a title, not a separate card type -- they count as Villains.

A card type found in the main deck. Super Powers grant Power and often have destroy or Defense abilities. Examples: Heat Vision (+3 Power, may destroy a card), Super Speed (draw a card, Defense), Bulletproof (+2 Power, Defense).

A card type found in the main deck. Equipment cards grant Power and often have Defense abilities. Examples: The Cape and Cowl (+2 Power, Defense: discard to avoid Attack, draw two), Nth Metal (+1 Power, look at top of deck, may destroy it), Power Ring (+3 Power, reveal top card for bonus).

A card type found in the main deck. When played, Locations stay face up in front of you permanently. Each has an Ongoing ability that triggers once per turn when you play a specific card type. Examples: The Batcave, Arkham Asylum, Titans Tower.
The card type for Punch and Vulnerability cards in each player's starting deck. Punches give +1 Power. Vulnerabilities have no effect. Neither has any VP value.
A Super Power card that costs 3 and gives +2 Power. Always available from its own stack (16 total). A reliable early-game purchase when the Line-Up is too expensive. Worth 1 VP.

A card that provides no Power and has no ability. Worth -1 VP at game end. Never bought -- only gained through Attack effects. 20 in the Weakness stack. If the stack runs out, effects that would give Weaknesses simply do not.
A Starter card that gives +1 Power when played. Each player starts with seven Punches. Worth 0 VP. The basic building block of your deck that you will eventually upgrade beyond.
A Starter card with no Power and no ability. Each player starts with three Vulnerabilities. Worth 0 VP but effectively negative because they are dead draws. Destroy them as soon as possible.

A card ability that affects all other players (called 'foes'). When you play an Attack, each opponent must suffer the effect unless they play a Defense card. Only one Defense card may be played per Attack. Common effects: gain Weakness, discard cards, reveal hand.

A card ability that lets you avoid an Attack. Discard the Defense card to negate the Attack for yourself only. Most Defense cards also give a bonus (e.g., draw cards). Only one Defense per Attack. Found on Super Power and Equipment cards like Super Speed and Bulletproof.

A keyword on Location cards indicating the effect triggers repeatedly. Ongoing abilities trigger once per turn when you play a specific card type for the first time that turn. The Location remains in play for the rest of the game.

An Attack printed on Super-Villain cards that triggers immediately when the Super-Villain is flipped face up on the stack. Affects all players (not during any player's turn). Can be defended against. Ra's al Ghul has no First Appearance Attack.

An oversized card dealt to each player at the start of the game. Provides a unique ability usable every turn at no cost. Seven available: Batman, Superman, Wonder Woman, The Flash, Green Lantern, Cyborg, and Aquaman.
Super Hero ability: +1 Power for each Equipment you play during your turn. Synergizes with Equipment-heavy decks and The Batcave Location.
Super Hero ability: The first time a card tells you to draw one or more cards during each of your turns, draw an additional card. Synergizes with card-draw effects.
Super Hero ability: If you play three or more cards with different names and cost 1 or more during your turn, +3 Power. Rewards deck diversity and varied card types.
Super Hero ability: You go first. The first time a card tells you to draw one or more cards during each of your turns, draw an additional card. Same draw bonus as Superman, plus always goes first.
Super Hero ability: If you play three or more cards with different names and cost 1 or more during your turn, +3 Power. Similar to Wonder Woman's ability, rewards card name variety.
Super Hero ability: If you play one or more Super Powers during your turn, +1 Power. If you play one or more Equipment during your turn, draw a card. Benefits from both card types.
Super Hero ability: Provides bonuses related to card costs. Available for games with more than five players or as an alternate hero choice.

Super-Villain. Always starts face up on top of the Super-Villain stack. Cost 8, worth 5 VP. Ability: +3 Power. At end of turn, goes to bottom of owner's deck (not discard pile). No First Appearance Attack.

Super-Villain. Cost 10, worth 10 VP. Ability: +2 Power, each foe chooses to discard a random card or let you draw a card. First Appearance Attack: each player passes a card left; if the received card costs 1+, gain a Weakness.
Super-Villain. Cost 10, worth 10 VP. Ability: Draw three cards. First Appearance Attack: each player gains a Weakness for each Villain in the Line-Up.
Super-Villain. Ability: Double your Power this turn. First Appearance Attack: each player reveals hand and discards all cards with cost 2 or less. Devastating First Appearance Attack.
Location card. Cost 1, worth 1 VP. Ongoing: When you play your first Equipment on each of your turns, draw a card. Essential for Batman and Equipment-heavy strategies.
Location card. Cost 5, worth 1 VP. Ongoing: When you play your first Villain on each of your turns, draw a card. Excellent for decks with multiple Villain cards.
Location card. Ongoing: When you play your first Hero on each of your turns, draw a card. Strong for Hero-heavy decks.
Super Power card. Cost 2, worth 2 VP. Ability: +3 Power. You may destroy a card in your hand or discard pile. One of the best cards for deck thinning.
Equipment card. Cost 3, worth 1 VP. Ability: +1 Power. Look at top card of your deck, you may destroy it. Useful for removing Vulnerabilities from the top of your deck.
Super Power card. Cost 3. Ability: Draw a card. Defense: discard to avoid Attack, draw two cards. Dual-purpose card with card draw and defensive capability.
Equipment card. Cost 4, worth 1 VP. Ability: +2 Power. Defense: discard to avoid Attack, draw two cards. Strong early buy for Power and protection.
Super Power card. Cost 4, worth 1 VP. Ability: +2 Power. Defense: discard to avoid Attack, draw a card, may destroy a card in your discard pile. Combines defense with deck thinning.
Hero card. Ability: Draw a card. Cheap hero that provides card draw, synergizing well with The Flash and Superman Super Hero abilities.
Six copies in the main deck. VP scales quadratically: 1 copy = 1 VP, 2 copies = 4 VP total, 3 = 9 VP, 4 = 16 VP, 5 = 25 VP, 6 = 36 VP. High-risk, high-reward collection strategy.
Villain card. Cost 5, worth 1 VP. Ability: +2 Power. Attack: each foe gains a Weakness. One of the strongest Attack cards for disrupting opponents.
Game term for other players affected by an Attack. In a standard game, all other players are foes. In the Team variant, your teammate is not a foe.

The process of playing through your entire deck, reshuffling, and drawing again. Each cycle, your purchased cards appear in your hand. Faster cycling (smaller deck) means seeing your best cards more often.
